driver.body: If Driver 1's rails are what make Kepler a payments company, the credit engine is what makes it a **fintech** [S-054]. Driver 2 takes the unified ledger built in Driver 1 and does the one thing an incumbent bank structurally cannot: it underwrites Kepler's 28.4m monthly actives on behavioural collateral — how reliably you ride, charge, deliver, and pay — rather than on a thin or absent credit file [S-054]. This is the hinge where the moat's trust term T stops being a soft virtue and becomes monetization, because trust that is measured can be lent against [S-054]. Every clean ride, on-time delivery, and settled charge is a data point the bureau never sees and Kepler already owns — so the collateral is not a document a user brings to the lender, it is the exhaust the platform has been collecting all along.

mechanisms.body:
The core mechanism is that Kepler scores creditworthiness from platform behaviour that is **exogenous to any credit bureau** [S-055]. A Driver 3 courier with 18 months of on-time deliveries and stable Driver 1 commuting patterns is a demonstrably better risk than her thin bureau file suggests — the file is silent in exactly the place where Kepler is loud [S-055]. This is the second cross-domain coupling in the flywheel: Payments underwrites on D1 mobility regularity and D3 logistics reliability, two signals a single-vertical lender can never assemble because it touches only one of the surfaces [S-055].

Trace S-405 documents the lift and it is not marginal — adding the cross-domain behavioural features cut the Gini-normalised default rate by 210bps at constant approval, or, read from the other side of the same frontier, raised approvals by 14% at constant loss [S-405]. The scoring logic physically lives in Driver 6's feature store but is owned, productised, and priced by Driver 4 — D6 supplies the substrate, D4 owns the P&L [S-055].

Trace S-406 is the query itself, and the load-bearing detail is the thick_file flag: a user is "thick" not because a bureau holds data on them but because Kepler does, across domains [S-406]. That single boolean is the entire underwriting moat compressed to one line of the scoring query.

The score from Mechanism 2.1 powers three distinct products: courier working-capital advances (D3), device instalments (D5), and consumer BNPL on rides and charges (D1/D2) [S-056]. The chaining between them is the whole point, not a footnote. A device instalment (D5) puts a proprietary-sensor device — one that raises the moat's data-breadth term B — into the hands of a user who could not have paid upfront, so **credit manufactures moat inputs for other domains** rather than merely earning a spread on the loan [S-056].

Trace S-407 quantifies that feed directly: instalment financing raised D5 device attach among the bottom two income quartiles by 31%, and that attach flows straight into D5's breadth term [S-407]. Read the sequence and the accounting inverts — credit here is not a yield product to be optimised on its own net-interest-margin; it is a distribution subsidy for the moat, repaid not in interest but in better data across the other five domains [S-056].

Underwriting on behaviour is powerful and **pro-cyclical**: the same D1 and D3 signals that predict good times can turn together in a downturn [S-057]. Driver 2 therefore hands a specific risk downstream to Driver 3 — correlated behavioural default, where a whole cohort deteriorates at once rather than as independent draws [S-057].

Trace S-408 sets the guardrail: exposure is capped per cohort and provisioned against a stressed scenario in which D1 ride frequency and D3 job volume fall together — the very correlation the cross-domain data itself reveals, turned from an edge into a stress input [S-408]. That is the honest tension at the centre of the thesis: the credit engine's edge and its systemic risk come from the same source [S-057]. The cross-domain signal that lets Kepler lend where banks cannot is the same signal that co-moves in a downturn, so the discipline is to price and provision for the correlation rather than to pretend the behavioural collateral is diversified when it is not [S-408].
